rationale: The two papers are tightly aligned on the same core constructs (hedonic vs. utilitarian consumption/attitudes): the citing paper develops a HED/UT attitude scale, while the cited paper is a major empirical/theoretical study of hedonic–utilitarian choice asymmetries. Given this conceptual overlap and exact DOI-matched reference, the citation could plausibly serve as theory/mechanism grounding rather than a purely incidental mention, so it should be kept for full relation judgment.
